title: Renewing Your Mortgage — A 90-Day Playbook
metaDescription: A clear 90-day plan for renewing your Canadian mortgage. What to do at 120, 90, 60 and 30 days before maturity to lock the best rate and terms.
intro: Most renewals get rushed. Here's a calm, week-by-week plan starting 120 days out — the same one I run for every client whose term is ending.

body.heading: 120 days out: take stock
paragraphs: Your lender will mail a renewal letter around now. Open it but don't sign it. Pull your latest mortgage statement, check the outstanding balance, the remaining amortization and the maturity date. Forward both to me.

body.heading: 90 days out: lock something
paragraphs: We compare your lender's offer to current market rates and pick the right move — renegotiate, switch, or refinance if you also want to take cash out. We lock a rate hold; this protects you against rate increases for the next 90 days. If rates fall during the hold, most lenders float you down.

body.heading: 60 days out: paperwork
paragraphs: If you're switching, the new lender prepares discharge instructions and your file is fully underwritten. If you're staying, your renewal documents are signed and the new term begins automatically on maturity day.

body.heading: 30 days out: confirm and breathe
paragraphs: Final confirmation of payment amounts, frequency, and PAD setup. Funds are positioned. On maturity day, the new mortgage takes over — same address, possibly a new lender, and a rate that you actually negotiated.

faqs.question: What if I miss the 120-day window?
faqs.answer: Don't worry — anytime in the last 90 days still gives you negotiating room. Below 30 days, options narrow because some lenders need more time to fund a switch. Below 14 days, you're usually stuck with whatever your current lender offers.

faqs.question: Can I extend my amortization at renewal?
faqs.answer: Renewing alone keeps the existing amortization. To extend, you'd refinance — different paperwork, potentially different rates and qualifying. We discuss whether that makes sense based on your goals.
